title: About
text:
Research Interests
Broadly speaking, I am interested in the applications of mathematical analysis, topology, and geometry in quantitative finance. Some specific topics of interest are:

Information geometry
\(H^\infty\) optimization
Numerical methods for free-boundary partial differential equations

title: Your Name
text:
I’m a first year PhD student interested in the applications of complex analysis and differential geometry to quantitative finance.
I write about these topics on my blog.

title: Hardy Spaces, Inner-Outer Factorizations, and Finance
text:
Recall that the Hardy space \(H^2(\mathbb{D})\) consists of all analytic functions \(f\) on the unit disc \(\mathbb{D}\) satisfying
\[
\sup_{0 < r < 1} \int_0^{2\pi} |f(re^{i\theta})|^2 \, \frac{d\theta}{2\pi} < \infty.
\]
A foundational result is the inner-outer factorization: every \(f \in H^2\) can be written uniquely as
\[
f = \Theta \cdot g
\]
where \(\Theta\) is an inner function (\(|\Theta(e^{i\theta})| = 1\) a.e.) and \(g\) is an outer function:
\[
g(z) = c \exp\left( \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi}
\frac{e^{i\theta} + z}{e^{i\theta} - z}
\log |f(e^{i\theta})| \, d\theta \right).
\]


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Visualize a simple Blaschke factor (inner function)
def blaschke_factor(z, a):
    """Single Blaschke factor with zero at a."""
    return (z - a) / (1 - np.conj(a) * z)

theta = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 500)
z_boundary = np.exp(1j * theta)

a = 0.5 + 0.3j  # zero inside the disc

B_values = blaschke_factor(z_boundary, a)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

# |B(e^{i theta})| = 1 on the boundary
axes[0].plot(theta, np.abs(B_values))
axes[0].set_title(r'$|B(e^{i\theta})|$ on $\partial\mathbb{D}$')
axes[0].set_xlabel(r'$\theta$')
axes[0].set_ylim(0.95, 1.05)
axes[0].axhline(y=1, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

# Phase of B on the boundary
axes[1].plot(theta, np.angle(B_values))
axes[1].set_title(r'$\arg B(e^{i\theta})$')
axes[1].set_xlabel(r'$\theta$')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Hardy Spaces, Inner-Outer Factorizations, and Finance
text:
Recall that the Hardy space \(H^2(\mathbb{D})\) consists of all analytic functions \(f\) on the unit disc \(\mathbb{D}\) satisfying
\[
\sup_{0 < r < 1} \int_0^{2\pi} |f(re^{i\theta})|^2 \, \frac{d\theta}{2\pi} < \infty.
\]
A foundational result is the inner-outer factorization: every \(f \in H^2\) can be written uniquely as
\[
f = \Theta \cdot g
\]
where \(\Theta\) is an inner function (\(|\Theta(e^{i\theta})| = 1\) a.e.) and \(g\) is an outer function:
\[
g(z) = c \exp\left( \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi}
\frac{e^{i\theta} + z}{e^{i\theta} - z}
\log |f(e^{i\theta})| \, d\theta \right).
\]


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Visualize a simple Blaschke factor (inner function)
def blaschke_factor(z, a):
    """Single Blaschke factor with zero at a."""
    return (z - a) / (1 - np.conj(a) * z)

theta = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 500)
z_boundary = np.exp(1j * theta)

a = 0.5 + 0.3j  # zero inside the disc

B_values = blaschke_factor(z_boundary, a)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

# |B(e^{i theta})| = 1 on the boundary
axes[0].plot(theta, np.abs(B_values))
axes[0].set_title(r'$|B(e^{i\theta})|$ on $\partial\mathbb{D}$')
axes[0].set_xlabel(r'$\theta$')
axes[0].set_ylim(0.95, 1.05)
axes[0].axhline(y=1, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

# Phase of B on the boundary
axes[1].plot(theta, np.angle(B_values))
axes[1].set_title(r'$\arg B(e^{i\theta})$')
axes[1].set_xlabel(r'$\theta$')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
